content.text: High-throughput, durable event streaming sits at the foundation of any agentic-AI system that needs audit trails, multi-consumer fan-out, or the ability to replay the exact sequence of decisions and tool invocations a running agent made. Apache Kafka delivers exactly these capabilities: it stores events as an ordered, append-only log, partitions that log for parallel throughput, and lets many independent consumers read at their own pace without interfering with one another or with the producers writing new events.
Apache Kafka is a distributed commit log originally developed at LinkedIn and open-sourced in 2011. It models data as a sequence of immutable records written to named
. Each topic is split into one or more

— ordered, numbered sequences of records stored durably on disk. Producers append records to the end of a partition; consumers read records starting from any offset in the sequence and track their progress by committing the offset of the last record they successfully processed.
Unlike traditional message brokers that delete a message once it is delivered, Kafka retains records for a configurable period (time-based or size-based). This makes Kafka a

rather than a queue: every event is permanently available until retention expires, any number of consumer groups can read independently, and a consumer that falls behind or crashes can simply re-read from its last committed offset.
Modern Kafka runs without ZooKeeper using the
consensus protocol (Kafka 3.3+). This simplifies deployment significantly — a single process contains both the broker and metadata management — while keeping the same producer and consumer APIs.

Agent systems are fundamentally event-driven: every LLM call, tool invocation, state transition, and retry decision is a discrete event that has a time, a cause, and downstream effects. Without a durable central record of those events, debugging a failing multi-step agent requires reconstructing what happened from fragmented logs across multiple services — a slow, error-prone process.

Kafka solves this by giving every component in an agentic platform a single place to publish events, and every downstream consumer — audit database writers, metric aggregators, billing counters, debug replayers — a single, consistent place to consume those events. Because each consumer group maintains its own read position independently, adding a new analytics consumer does not require any change to the orchestrator code, and a slow billing consumer does not slow down the hot production path.

For regulated industries (finance, healthcare, legal-tech) the immutable log is also a compliance asset: it provides a tamper-evident record of every action the agent took, including what data it accessed and what instructions it was given. This record can be archived to object storage (S3, GCS) for multi-year retention at low cost, while Kafka brokers retain only recent events at high-performance NVMe storage.
— records persist on disk with configurable replication (
is typical). Consumers can seek to any offset and replay the full history of events, enabling retroactive analytics, debugging of past failures, or re-running downstream pipelines after a bug fix.
— partitioning distributes write and read load across multiple broker nodes and multiple consumer instances. Production deployments routinely sustain millions of messages per second.
— one producer topic can serve ten independent consumer groups simultaneously. Adding a new consumer (e.g., a real-time alerting service) requires zero changes to the producer.
— records published with the same key always land in the same partition, guaranteeing ordering within a key. Partitioning by
ensures all events for a single agent run appear in sequence.
— Kafka's idempotent producer combined with transactional APIs provides end-to-end exactly-once processing in pipelines that use Kafka Streams or Flink.

— Kafka Connect provides hundreds of off-the-shelf source and sink connectors (Postgres, S3, Elasticsearch, BigQuery). Kafka Streams and Flink run stateful streaming computations directly on Kafka topics.

— even with KRaft, Kafka requires careful capacity planning: partition counts, replication factors, retention sizes, and consumer group lag monitoring all need attention. Fully managed offerings (Confluent Cloud, MSK, Aiven) reduce but do not eliminate this burden.

— Kafka has no built-in concept of job ownership, priority, or at-most-once delivery. Implementing "one worker claims one job" on top of Kafka requires extra logic (partition assignment, explicit claim messages, or a separate locking layer). For simple job dispatch, RabbitMQ or Celery fit more naturally.
Higher tail latency than in-memory brokers
— Kafka writes to disk and flushes to followers before acknowledging (when
). Median publish latency is in the 2–10 ms range; p99 latency can be 50–100 ms under peak load. Redis pub/sub and RabbitMQ have sub-millisecond median latency for small payloads.

— consumers must handle duplicate delivery unless they implement idempotent processing or use Kafka's transactional API. A consumer that crashes after processing but before committing its offset will re-process the same records on restart.
— adding partitions later disrupts key-to-partition routing. Choose the partition count for expected peak throughput before launching, using the

— default max is 1 MB per record. LLM context windows can be hundreds of kilobytes of tokens; embedding full transcripts in Kafka records is expensive and risks hitting this limit. Store pointers to object storage instead.
is a logical category for related events (e.g.,
partitions. Within a partition, every record has a monotonically increasing
starting from 0. Producers choose a partition based on the record key (consistent hashing) or round-robin if no key is supplied.
split partitions among themselves — each partition is assigned to exactly one group member at a time. If a member crashes, Kafka triggers a
and redistributes that member's partitions to the survivors. A consumer group with
members can efficiently parallelize up to
partitions; additional members beyond that count sit idle.
broker that handles all reads and writes, and one or more
brokers that replicate the leader's log. The leader tracks which followers are caught up via the
set. A record is considered committed once all ISR brokers have written it. Setting
on the producer ensures the client only receives an acknowledgement after ISR replication, preventing data loss even if the leader fails immediately after acknowledging.
Consumers store their committed offsets in the internal Kafka topic
record to fetch, not the last record processed. Setting

(manual commit) and committing only after successfully processing (and durably writing) each record is the safest pattern: if the process crashes between processing and committing, the record is re-fetched and re-processed rather than silently lost.
A topic can be configured with
policy. In compact mode, Kafka retains only the most recent record for each key indefinitely, discarding older records with the same key. This is useful for agent configuration topics keyed by
: each agent's latest configuration is always available, without unbounded log growth.

consensus protocol replaces ZooKeeper for storing cluster metadata. A subset of brokers are elected as KRaft controllers and maintain the authoritative metadata log. This eliminates the operational overhead of running a separate ZooKeeper cluster and reduces broker startup time from minutes to seconds for large clusters.

The diagram below shows how Kafka sits between the producer services (orchestrator, tool executor, LLM wrapper) and the consumer services (audit, metrics, billing, replay) in a typical agentic-AI deployment. Multiple partitions within each topic allow both producers and consumers to scale horizontally. A Schema Registry enforces the contract between producers and consumers as event schemas evolve over time.

attrs.alt: Kafka architecture diagram for an agentic-AI system showing producers, broker cluster with partitions, consumer groups, and long-term S3 sink

content.text: (teal) — high-volume stream of every tool call, LLM call, and state transition, partitioned by
to preserve ordering per agent run.
(blue) — low-volume command stream for dispatch, cancel, and retry signals, often partitioned by
(purple) — errors and dead-letter events that need human review or automated retry logic.
Each consumer group reads all partitions independently; a slow consumer (e.g., billing) cannot block the audit writer.
A Kafka Connect S3 sink exports events to object storage for long-term archival without loading broker disks.
Multiple independent services need to consume the same stream of agent events without coupling to each other.
You need a durable, replayable audit trail for compliance, debugging, or model evaluation.
Event volume exceeds what a single database write path can absorb (>10,000 events/sec sustained).
You need retroactive processing — running a new analytics pipeline over historical events without re-running the agents.
You are building event-sourced agent state, where the agent's state is the result of replaying its event log.
You need ordering guarantees per agent run — partitioning by
ensures that all events for a run are processed in order.
You need a simple background job queue with priority, retries, and exactly-one-worker semantics — use Celery, RabbitMQ, or Temporal instead.
Your team is small and the operational overhead of running a Kafka cluster (even managed) is disproportionate to the traffic volume.
You need sub-millisecond publish latency for a tight feedback loop — Redis pub/sub or an in-process event bus will perform better.
You have only one consumer group and no need for replay — a simpler message broker or database queue suffices.
Step 1: Start Kafka with Docker Compose
The fastest way to run Kafka locally for development uses Confluent's all-in-one Docker image, which bundles the broker and Schema Registry:

# docker-compose.yml
version: "3.9"
services:
  kafka:
    image: confluentinc/cp-kafka:7.6.0
    ports:
      - "9092:9092"
    environment:
      KAFKA_NODE_ID: 1
      KAFKA_PROCESS_ROLES: broker,controller
      KAFKA_CONTROLLER_QUORUM_VOTERS: "1@kafka:9093"
      KAFKA_LISTENERS: PLAINTEXT://:9092,CONTROLLER://:9093
      KAFKA_ADVERTISED_LISTENERS: PLAINTEXT://localhost:9092
      KAFKA_CONTROLLER_LISTENER_NAMES: CONTROLLER
      KAFKA_LOG_DIRS: /var/lib/kafka/data
      CLUSTER_ID: "MkU3OEVBNTcwNTJENDM2Qk"

  schema-registry:
    image: confluentinc/cp-schema-registry:7.6.0
    ports:
      - "8081:8081"
    environment:
      SCHEMA_REGISTRY_KAFKASTORE_BOOTSTRAP_SERVERS: kafka:9092
      SCHEMA_REGISTRY_HOST_NAME: schema-registry
Create topics before running producers. Setting partition count at topic creation is important because it cannot be reduced later:

# Create the three standard agent topics
kafka-topics --bootstrap-server localhost:9092 --create \
  --topic agent.events --partitions 12 --replication-factor 1

kafka-topics --bootstrap-server localhost:9092 --create \
  --topic agent.cmds --partitions 4 --replication-factor 1

kafka-topics --bootstrap-server localhost:9092 --create \
  --topic agent.errors --partitions 4 --replication-factor 1

# Verify
kafka-topics --bootstrap-server localhost:9092 --describe --topic agent.events
Step 3: Produce events from the orchestrator
Install the async Kafka client for Python:
Then publish agent events as the orchestrator runs:

# agent_producer.py
import asyncio
import json
import time
from aiokafka import AIOKafkaProducer
from aiokafka.errors import KafkaError

async def make_producer() -> AIOKafkaProducer:
    return AIOKafkaProducer(
        bootstrap_servers="localhost:9092",
        # Durability: wait for all ISR replicas before acking
        acks="all",
        # Prevent duplicate records on transient network errors
        enable_idempotence=True,
        # Batch small records together for throughput
        linger_ms=5,
        compression_type="lz4",
        value_serializer=lambda v: json.dumps(v).encode(),
        key_serializer=lambda k: k.encode() if k else None,
    )

async def emit(producer: AIOKafkaProducer, run_id: str, event: dict) -> None:
    event["ts"] = time.time()
    event["run_id"] = run_id
    await producer.send_and_wait(
        "agent.events",
        value=event,
        key=run_id,          # partition key → all events for a run stay ordered
    )

async def main():
    producer = await make_producer()
    await producer.start()
    try:
        run_id = "run-abc-123"

        await emit(producer, run_id, {
            "type": "run.started",
            "agent": "research-agent-v2",
            "input_hash": "sha256:aabbcc…",
        })

        await emit(producer, run_id, {
            "type": "tool.invoked",
            "tool": "web_search",
            "args": {"query": "kafka consumer group rebalance"},
        })

        await emit(producer, run_id, {
            "type": "llm.called",
            "model": "claude-3-5-sonnet-20241022",
            "prompt_tokens": 1024,
            "max_tokens": 512,
        })

        await emit(producer, run_id, {
            "type": "run.finished",
            "status": "success",
            "duration_ms": 4200,
        })
    finally:
        await producer.stop()

asyncio.run(main())
Step 4: Consume with manual offset commit
Consumer groups allow multiple independent services to read the same topic:

# audit_consumer.py
import asyncio
import json
from aiokafka import AIOKafkaConsumer

async def write_audit(record: dict) -> None:
    # Replace with your actual database write
    print(f"[audit] {record}")

async def main():
    consumer = AIOKafkaConsumer(
        "agent.events",
        bootstrap_servers="localhost:9092",
        group_id="audit-writer",           # unique group = independent cursor
        enable_auto_commit=False,          # manual commit for safety
        auto_offset_reset="earliest",      # start from beginning if no committed offset
        value_deserializer=lambda b: json.loads(b.decode()),
    )
    await consumer.start()
    try:
        async for msg in consumer:
            try:
                await write_audit(msg.value)
                # Only commit AFTER the record has been durably written
                await consumer.commit()
            except Exception as exc:
                print(f"Failed to process offset {msg.offset}: {exc}")
                # Do not commit — record will be re-delivered on restart
    finally:
        await consumer.stop()

asyncio.run(main())
This minimal example demonstrates the core produce-consume loop using the synchronous
library, which is easier to read for newcomers than the async variant:

# simple_kafka.py  — run with:  pip install kafka-python
from kafka import KafkaProducer, KafkaConsumer
import json, time

BOOTSTRAP = "localhost:9092"
TOPIC = "hello.kafka"

# --- Producer ---
producer = KafkaProducer(
    bootstrap_servers=BOOTSTRAP,
    value_serializer=lambda v: json.dumps(v).encode(),
    key_serializer=lambda k: k.encode(),
)

for i in range(5):
    event = {"seq": i, "msg": f"hello #{i}", "ts": time.time()}
    producer.send(TOPIC, key="demo", value=event)
    print(f"Produced: {event}")
producer.flush()
producer.close()

# --- Consumer (reads same 5 records) ---
consumer = KafkaConsumer(
    TOPIC,
    bootstrap_servers=BOOTSTRAP,
    group_id="demo-group",
    auto_offset_reset="earliest",
    consumer_timeout_ms=3000,          # stop after 3 s of no new messages
    value_deserializer=lambda b: json.loads(b.decode()),
)

for record in consumer:
    print(f"offset={record.offset} key={record.key} value={record.value}")
consumer.close()
Example 2: Agent event pipeline with dead-letter routing (realistic)
In production, failed records should be forwarded to a dead-letter topic rather than blocking the consumer. The following pattern does this:

# pipeline_consumer.py
import asyncio, json, time
from aiokafka import AIOKafkaConsumer, AIOKafkaProducer

BOOTSTRAP = "localhost:9092"
SOURCE_TOPIC = "agent.events"
DLQ_TOPIC = "agent.errors"
GROUP_ID = "metrics-aggregator"
MAX_RETRIES = 3

async def process_event(event: dict) -> None:
    """Real implementation would push metrics to Prometheus or InfluxDB."""
    if event.get("type") == "llm.called":
        tokens = event.get("prompt_tokens", 0) + event.get("max_tokens", 0)
        # push_counter("llm_tokens_total", tokens, labels={"model": event["model"]})
        print(f"  → counted {tokens} tokens for model {event['model']}")

async def main():
    consumer = AIOKafkaConsumer(
        SOURCE_TOPIC,
        bootstrap_servers=BOOTSTRAP,
        group_id=GROUP_ID,
        enable_auto_commit=False,
        auto_offset_reset="latest",
        value_deserializer=lambda b: json.loads(b.decode()),
    )
    producer = AIOKafkaProducer(
        bootstrap_servers=BOOTSTRAP,
        value_serializer=lambda v: json.dumps(v).encode(),
    )

    await consumer.start()
    await producer.start()
    try:
        async for msg in consumer:
            event = msg.value
            attempt = 0
            while attempt < MAX_RETRIES:
                try:
                    await process_event(event)
                    break
                except Exception as exc:
                    attempt += 1
                    if attempt >= MAX_RETRIES:
                        dlq_record = {
                            "original_topic": msg.topic,
                            "original_offset": msg.offset,
                            "original_partition": msg.partition,
                            "error": str(exc),
                            "event": event,
                            "failed_at": time.time(),
                        }
                        await producer.send_and_wait(
                            DLQ_TOPIC,
                            value=dlq_record,
                            key=event.get("run_id", "").encode(),
                        )
                    else:
                        await asyncio.sleep(0.2 * (2 ** attempt))  # exponential backoff
            await consumer.commit()
    finally:
        await consumer.stop()
        await producer.stop()

asyncio.run(main())

A production research-automation platform runs hundreds of concurrent agent tasks, each composed of 5–40 LLM calls and 10–200 tool invocations per run. At peak hours this generates roughly 50,000 events per minute across all runs combined.

. Replication factor is 3 across 3 broker nodes running on i3.2xlarge EC2 instances (NVMe storage). This configuration provides 3× durability and can sustain 200,000 events/sec sustained write throughput with
Four independent consumer groups read the topic simultaneously:

— writes every event row to DynamoDB within 200 ms of production. Compliance requires a 7-year retention window; a Kafka Connect S3 sink exports daily partitions to S3 Glacier after 30 days on-broker.
— computes per-model token throughput, per-tool invocation rate, and p99 latency histograms and pushes them to a Prometheus pushgateway every 10 seconds.
— maintains running token and tool-call counts per tenant in Redis for real-time usage metering.
— available only in staging. Operators can seek this consumer to any offset and replay a specific run's events against a mock LLM to reproduce bugs without re-running live agents.

Schema evolution is managed through Confluent Schema Registry using JSON Schema with backward-compatible required-field additions. The registry enforces that producers cannot publish events that would break existing consumers, catching schema errors at development time rather than in production.

Kafka consumer group lag is monitored with Burrow and alerting fires if any consumer group falls more than 60 seconds behind, which would indicate a processing bottleneck or outage in one of the downstream services.
Mistake 1: Using Kafka as a task queue

Kafka does not natively support per-job ownership. If five consumer instances read from three partitions, each partition is owned by one consumer — there is no way to claim a specific record before processing it starts. Developers sometimes try to implement task-queue semantics on Kafka and end up with complex offset management, in-flight tracking tables, and race conditions. The right tool for "one agent handles one job" dispatch is a purpose-built task queue: Celery with Redis/RabbitMQ backend, or Temporal for durable workflow execution.
If Kafka must be used as both event log and dispatch queue (to minimize infrastructure), the typical pattern is: publish a
event to Kafka; a single dispatcher consumer reads it and pushes the job into a proper task queue. Kafka handles fan-out to analytics consumers; the task queue handles execution ownership.
Mistake 2: Committing offsets before processing is durable

# WRONG — offsets committed before the write succeeds
async for msg in consumer:
    await consumer.commit()        # ← committed here
    await write_to_database(msg)   # ← if this fails, event is silently lost

# CORRECT — commit only after the durable write
async for msg in consumer:
    await write_to_database(msg)   # ← durable write first
    await consumer.commit()        # ← then commit
Mistake 3: Embedding large payloads in Kafka records

Kafka's default message size limit is 1 MB. LLM full-context transcripts, tool result blobs, or agent memory snapshots can easily exceed this. Beyond the size limit, large records also waste broker disk, slow consumer throughput, and increase serialization overhead. The correct pattern is the
pattern: publish a pointer (e.g., an S3 object key or database primary key) in the Kafka record and store the large payload separately.

Partition count determines the maximum parallelism for both producers and consumers. Increasing partition count after initial deployment does not relocate existing data — existing keys will map to different partitions, breaking ordering guarantees. A common rule of thumb for agent event streams: set partition count to 3–5× the expected maximum consumer instances at peak to give headroom for horizontal scaling without repartitioning.
Mistake 5: Not monitoring consumer group lag

A consumer group that is falling behind accumulates lag. If a consumer crashes or is deployed with a bug, lag can grow to millions of records before the issue is noticed. Without lag monitoring (Burrow, Kafka UI, or the
command), a billing or audit consumer could be hours behind without any visible alert.

— enforce a shared schema (Avro or JSON Schema) for each topic. The registry rejects incompatible schema changes at the producer side before they reach consumers. Use backward-compatible evolution: add fields with defaults, never remove required fields.
Separate topics by volume and retention needs
at 4 partitions with 90-day retention. Mixing different retention requirements in one topic means the most conservative policy wins.
Set acks=all and enable_idempotence=True on producers
— prevents data loss on leader failure and duplicate records on transient network retries. The throughput cost is modest (5–15%) and the durability gain is significant.
and commit only after successful, durable processing. Auto-commit fires on a timer and can commit offsets for records that were fetched but not yet processed.
— alert when any group exceeds a lag threshold corresponding to your acceptable processing delay (e.g., 60 seconds for audit, 5 minutes for billing).
Keep records small — use the claim-check pattern
for payloads above ~50 KB. Aim for records under 10 KB on the hot event path.
Use log compaction for agent configuration topics
— topics that store the latest state per agent (configuration, capability registry) should use
so consumers can always reconstruct current state by reading the full compacted topic.
— target 10–50 MB/sec throughput per partition. Divide your expected peak event rate by 30 MB/sec (conservative) to get the minimum partition count.

Kafka's throughput scales nearly linearly with the number of partitions and broker nodes. A single Kafka broker on commodity hardware (i3.2xlarge, NVMe) can sustain ~200–400 MB/sec write throughput. A three-broker cluster with 12 partitions can typically absorb 600 MB/sec combined. For an agentic-AI platform, each event is 200–2,000 bytes, meaning three brokers can handle 300,000–3,000,000 events/sec — far beyond what most agent deployments need.
End-to-end produce-to-consume latency depends on three settings:
— how long the producer waits to batch records before sending. Higher values improve throughput; lower values reduce latency. For agent event streams,
— requires all ISR replicas to acknowledge before the producer gets a response. This adds one additional network RTT (5–10 ms on a well-configured cluster) compared to
— controls how long the broker waits to accumulate data before responding to a consumer fetch. Lower values reduce consumer latency at the cost of more network requests.

For agentic-AI workloads, typical end-to-end latency is 10–50 ms from producer send to consumer receipt under normal load, which is acceptable for audit and analytics use cases. If sub-5 ms latency is required for a hot path (e.g., real-time safety filtering), consider Redis pub/sub or an in-process event bus instead.

Scale consumer throughput by adding consumer instances up to the partition count. For a topic with 12 partitions, deploy up to 12 consumer pods; the Kafka consumer group protocol automatically assigns one partition per pod. Beyond 12 pods, additional instances remain idle. If the topic needs more parallelism, the only option is to increase the partition count on a new topic and migrate producers.
Retention policy is set per-topic with

(size). For high-volume agent event streams, 30-day retention on NVMe storage typically requires 500 GB–2 TB per broker for a well-sized cluster. Use Kafka's tiered storage feature (available in Confluent Platform and MSK) or a Kafka Connect S3 sink to offload older segments to object storage, reducing broker disk requirements by 5–10×.
(Python) — async Kafka producer and consumer built on asyncio. Best fit for async agent frameworks (FastAPI, LangGraph, CrewAI).
(Python) — synchronous client, simpler for scripts and one-shot tools.
(Python) — wraps the high-performance C librdkafka library; higher throughput than pure-Python clients, recommended for production.
(Node.js) — idiomatic async Kafka client for JavaScript/TypeScript agent stacks.

# Check consumer group lag for all groups on a topic
kafka-consumer-groups --bootstrap-server localhost:9092 \
  --describe --group audit-writer

# Output includes: PARTITION  CURRENT-OFFSET  LOG-END-OFFSET  LAG
# LAG = LOG-END-OFFSET - CURRENT-OFFSET

# Reset consumer group offset to earliest (replay all events)
kafka-consumer-groups --bootstrap-server localhost:9092 \
  --group replay-group --topic agent.events \
  --reset-offsets --to-earliest --execute

For production monitoring, Kafka UI (open-source, available as a Docker image) provides a browser-based interface for viewing topic offsets, consumer group lag, and broker health without requiring CLI access.
When agent events need aggregation or enrichment in real-time, two mature frameworks integrate natively with Kafka:
(Java/Scala) — runs stateful stream processing inside the same JVM as the application, with no separate cluster required. Good for per-run aggregations and windowed counts.
— separate cluster, richer state management, handles exactly-once semantics with Kafka sources/sinks. Well-suited for complex CEP rules on agent event streams.
— fully managed Kafka with Schema Registry, ksqlDB, and tiered storage. Serverless option removes partition sizing entirely.
— managed Kafka on AWS with IAM authentication and MSK Serverless option.
— managed Kafka across AWS, GCP, Azure, and DigitalOcean with built-in Kafka Connect and Schema Registry.
— Kafka-API-compatible broker written in C++; lower latency and simpler operations (no JVM, no ZooKeeper/KRaft overhead). Drop-in replacement for most Kafka clients.
The table below compares Kafka against the most common alternatives for agent event streaming and messaging in agentic-AI systems:
In production, Kafka brokers must not be accessible without authentication. Two mechanisms are most common:
— username/password authentication stored in ZooKeeper or KRaft metadata. Easiest to configure for self-managed clusters.

— client certificates identify both clients and brokers. Required for compliance-sensitive deployments (PCI, HIPAA). Every producer and consumer presents a client certificate; brokers validate it against a trusted CA.
Authorization is managed through Kafka's ACL system (
) or, for fine-grained per-topic permissions, Confluent Role-Based Access Control. At minimum, producer services should only have
access to their specific topics, and consumer services should only have
Enable TLS encryption in transit by configuring

on all clients. Kafka does not encrypt data at rest by default — use encrypted volumes (AWS EBS encryption, LUKS) at the infrastructure layer. For extremely sensitive data (PII, API keys), encrypt the record value at the application layer before publishing, so the Kafka broker never sees plaintext.
, the cluster tolerates one broker failure with no data loss and no downtime. Producers will retry automatically when the leader re-elects.
, a consumer crash causes at-most the records in the current fetch batch to be re-processed. Design consumers to be idempotent so re-processing is safe.
(default when idempotent), the producer retries indefinitely and the broker deduplicates repeated sends using a sequence number. A single record is never written twice.

— Kafka elects a new leader from the ISR set automatically. Client libraries retry transparently after a brief pause (typically under 500 ms for modern clusters). Design event handlers to tolerate short produce delays without failing the entire agent run.
Agent audit logs published to Kafka must be tamper-evident. Strategies include:
Use HMAC signing of record values before publishing: consumers verify the signature before writing to the audit database.
, Kafka 3.9+) to detect corruption at rest.
Export records to S3 using Kafka Connect and enable S3 Object Lock (WORM) for the audit prefix to prevent deletion.
Kafka is a distributed append-only log, not a message queue — records are retained durably on disk and can be replayed by any number of independent consumer groups at any time.
to preserve event ordering per agent run; choose the partition count before launch because repartitioning breaks key routing.
on producers, and manual offset commit with
on consumers, to prevent both data loss and duplicate processing.

Kafka excels at audit trails, multi-consumer fan-out, event sourcing, and retroactive analytics — use a purpose-built task queue (Celery, RabbitMQ, Temporal) alongside Kafka for job dispatch and work assignment.
Apply the claim-check pattern for large payloads: publish a pointer to S3 or a database record in the Kafka event rather than embedding multi-KB blobs directly.
Monitor consumer group lag continuously — a silent accumulation of millions of unprocessed events is the most common operational failure mode in Kafka deployments.
Schema Registry enforcement and topic separation by volume and retention are the two most impactful best practices for long-running production systems.
Kafka's security model requires explicit configuration: enable SASL/mTLS for authentication, TLS for encryption in transit, and Kafka ACLs to limit each service to the topics it actually needs.
